currentSnapshot.definition: Showing a lack of common sense or judgement; absurd and foolish.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Applied affectionately or dismissively depending on context. 'Silly season' (summer news lull) shows a residual 'trivial' sense.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: Don't be silly — you can't carry all those bags at once.

historicalSnapshots.usageNote: From Old English 'sælig', meaning 'blessed by God'. The word carried the highest positive valence — to be silly was to be in God's favour.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Innocent; harmless; pitiable; simple (without negative connotation).
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The 'blessed' sense widened to include the innocent and the helpless — 'a silly child' meant a pitiable or defenceless child, not a foolish one.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The silly sheep wandered from the flock.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Feeble of mind; lacking good judgement; simple to the point of being easily deceived.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The 'innocent/helpless' sense darkened — to be simple and easily led became associated with foolishness. Shakespeare used it in this transitional register.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Absurd; trivially foolish; lacking sense or substance.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Fully pejorated. 'Silly' now reliably conveyed frivolity and lack of intellect, though still milder than 'idiotic' or 'stupid'.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: What a silly notion — you cannot drain the Thames with a teacup.

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From 'blessed by God' to 'foolishly trivial' — one of the most dramatic reversals in English.
summaryOfChange.longSummary: Few words in English have fallen further than 'silly'. In Old English 'sælig' was a term of the highest spiritual praise: to be silly was to be blessed by God. Through Middle English the sense widened to 'innocent and helpless', then darkened — the innocent became the simple-minded, the simple-minded became the easily-gulled, and by Early Modern English the word had fully pejorated into 'foolish'. Today it sits at the mild end of a spectrum of intellectual dismissal, all memory of its blessed origins lost to most speakers.

keyDates.label: Old English 'sælig'
keyDates.significance: The word in its original form meant 'blessed/happy' — cognate with German 'selig' (blessed), which still retains the positive meaning today.

keyDates.label: Shift to 'innocent/pitiable'
keyDates.significance: Middle English broadened the sense from 'blessed' to 'innocent and helpless' — the first step in the downward drift.

keyDates.label: Shakespeare's usage
keyDates.significance: By Shakespeare's era 'silly' could mean 'simple-minded' or 'easily deceived', marking the transition to its modern pejorative sense.

keyDates.label: Pejoration complete
keyDates.significance: By the mid-18th century dictionaries recorded 'silly' exclusively as 'foolish' or 'trivial', the blessed origin fully forgotten in common use.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — silly, adj.
sources.attribution: Paraphrase and excerpt. OED content © Oxford University Press. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: Originally: blessed, happy, fortunate. Later: innocent, harmless; simple, unsophisticated. Now chiefly: foolish, showing a lack of sense or judgement.
sources.relevanceNote: The OED's historical quotations trace the full arc from 'blessed' to 'foolish' across twelve centuries.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — silly, adj. (modern senses)
sources.attribution: Paraphrase. OED content © Oxford University Press.
sources.excerpt: Exhibiting or characterized by a lack of common sense, judgement, or sobriety; foolish, senseless.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the fully pejorated modern sense.

sources.title: An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary
sources.author: Joseph Bosworth, T. Northcote Toller
sources.excerpt: sælig: happy, fortunate, blessed; spiritually prosperous.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the original Old English 'blessed' meaning, cognate with German 'selig'.

sources.title: Middle English Dictionary — seli/seely
sources.attribution: © University of Michigan. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: seli: (1) blessed, happy; (2) innocent, pitiable, weak; (3) simple, foolish.
sources.relevanceNote: Shows all three stages of meaning coexisting in Middle English texts.

relatedWorks.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream
relatedWorks.creator: William Shakespeare
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Contains uses of 'silly' in its transitional phase — sometimes meaning 'simple/harmless', sometimes 'foolish' — illustrating the word mid-drift.

relatedWorks.title: Comparative note: German 'selig'
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: German 'selig' (blessed, blissful) is a direct cognate of Old English 'sælig' and has retained the original positive meaning, providing a living counterpoint to the English pejoration.

relatedPages.title: Pejoration
relatedPages.summary: How and why word meanings become more negative over time.

relatedPages.title: Semantic Drift
relatedPages.summary: The broad phenomenon of word meanings shifting across time.

relatedConcepts.label: sælig (Old English)
relatedConcepts.note: The Old English root, still preserved in German 'selig' (blessed).

relatedConcepts.label: awful
relatedConcepts.note: Underwent similar pejoration: from 'awe-inspiring' to 'very bad'.

relatedConcepts.label: nice
relatedConcepts.note: Also began with a negative sense ('foolish') before ameliorating — the mirror journey.

ambiguityNotes: In cricket, 'silly mid-on' uses 'silly' to mean 'dangerously close' — a survival of the 'foolishly exposed/vulnerable' sense.
The 'silly season' (slow news summer) preserves the 'trivial/insubstantial' sub-sense.
Affectionate uses ('you silly thing') are milder than dismissive ones ('what a silly idea') — register matters.

timelineEvents.title: Sælig: blessed by God
timelineEvents.summary: Old English 'sælig' meant divinely blessed and fortunate — one of the highest compliments in the language.

timelineEvents.title: Innocent and pitiable
timelineEvents.summary: The sense of 'blessed' drifted toward 'innocent and helpless' — 'silly sheep' or 'a silly child' meant vulnerable, not foolish.

timelineEvents.title: Shakespeare bridges the gap
timelineEvents.summary: In Shakespeare's plays, 'silly' wavers between 'simple/harmless' and 'foolish' — a word caught mid-drift.

timelineEvents.title: Pejoration complete
timelineEvents.summary: By the mid-18th century, 'silly' had stabilised as a mild term of intellectual dismissal. The blessed origin was forgotten.
